coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-modified ferrofluid for dispersive solid-phase extraction of phenolic compounds in water and milk samples
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Dezhi
Li, Xiaolan
Meng, Dongling
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: In this study, novel carbon quantum dots/oleic acid-coated Fe3O4 composites (Fe3O4-OA/CQDs) were successfully synthesized via a simple synthesis strategy and used to prepare the ferrofluid for the extraction and determination of phenolic compounds from water and pretreated milk samples followed by HPLC. The ferrofluid was composed of ionic liquids (ILs) and Fe3O4-OA/CQDs. With the introduction of CQDs, the ferrofluid showed good dispersing capability in aqueous samples, and the method exhibited lower relative standard deviations (RSDs) (1.1–2.1%), which indicated its good reproducibility. In addition, a proper carbon chain length for ILs might maintain the integrity of the ferrofluid during the extraction process and increase the extraction efficiency of the method. Under the optimum conditions, the limits of detection were in the range of 0.09–0.17μgL−1. And the intra-day and inter-day precision (RSD%) for the analytes at 20 and 100μgL−1 were in the range of 1.5–2.6% and 1.6–2.8%, respectively. The recoveries of bisphenols and 4-tert-octylphenol in water and milk samples were between 94.5 and 102.1%, and the RSDs were all in range of 1.7 to 4.1%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bisphenols and 4-tert-octylphenol
Carbon quantum dots
Ferrofluid
Hydrophobicity
Ionic liquids

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of the method in water and milk samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR spectra of CQDs, Fe3O4-OA, Fe3O4-OA/CQDs and [C8MIm]PF6-Fe3O4-OA/CQDs mixtures; (B) XPS analysis of Fe3O4-OA/CQDs.
The TEM images of Fe3O4-OA (A), CQDs (A, inset) and Fe3O4-OA/CQDs NPs (B).
(A) The effect of different ferrofluids; (B) The effect of different type of ILs on the extraction efficiencies;
Chromatograms of (a) blank milk samples, (b) milk spiked with 20μgL−1 of BPs and 4-TOP.
Comparison of the proposed method with other method for determination of phenolic compounds.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Dummy molecular imprinting and solid-phase extraction.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results for several water and milk samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-modified ferrofluid for dispersive solid-phase extraction of phenolic compounds in water and milk samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Environmental Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Environmental Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technology Centre, China Tobacco Guangxi Industrial Co., Ltd., Nanning, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China Tobacco Guangxi Industrial Co., Ltd.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, novel carbon quantum dots/oleic acid-coated Fe3O4 composites (Fe3O4-OA/CQDs) were successfully synthesized via a simple synthesis strategy and used to prepare the ferrofluid for the extraction and determination of phenolic compounds from water and pretreated milk samples followed by HPLC. The ferrofluid was composed of ionic liquids (ILs) and Fe3O4-OA/CQDs. With the introduction of CQDs, the ferrofluid showed good dispersing capability in aqueous samples, and the method exhibited lower relative standard deviations (RSDs) (1.1–2.1%), which indicated its good reproducibility. In addition, a proper carbon chain length for ILs might maintain the integrity of the ferrofluid during the extraction process and increase the extraction efficiency of the method. Under the optimum conditions, the limits of detection were in the range of 0.09–0.17μgL−1. And the intra-day and inter-day precision (RSD%) for the analytes at 20 and 100μgL−1 were in the range of 1.5–2.6% and 1.6–2.8%, respectively. The recoveries of bisphenols and 4-tert-octylphenol in water and milk samples were between 94.5 and 102.1%, and the RSDs were all in range of 1.7 to 4.1%.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol-A (BPA), bisphenol-AF (BPAF), tetrabromobisphenol-A (TBBPA) and 4-tert-octylphenol (4-TOP) are the main ingredients used for the production of synthetic plastic materials, adhesives, epoxy resins, drink and food packaging [1,2]. These phenolic compounds are considered as the endocrine disrupting compounds and have a serious impact on the humans and animals at low concentrations [3]. Due to the low concentrations of phenolic compounds existing in real samples, a sensitive, effective and rapid preconcentration method is necessary for the determination of BPs and 4-TOP prior to chromatographic analysis. Liquid phase microextraction (LPME), using a water-immiscible solvent as the extractant, is often employed for extraction and determination of phenolic compounds from complex matrices and can be selected as an alternative to traditional preparation techniques. Very recently, several extraction solvents, such as supercritical fluids, ionic liquids and supramolecular solvents, have been employed for the separation and preconcentration of trace levels of organic compounds [4–6]. ILs, as the eco-friendly extraction solvents, can be promising as they both have unique chemical and physical properties [7]. It has been widely used in dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction (DLLME) as an extraction solvent [7].

To facilitate the retrieval of the extracting solvent, researchers have combined extraction solvents with magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) [8–10]. The ferrofluid based on the combination of MNPs and carrier liquid can be used to overcome some inconveniences in retrieving extractant. Ferrofluids, as a colloidal system, are stable suspensions of MNPs suspended in a carrier liquid such as ester, water and hydrocarbons [11]. The main interesting features of ferrofluids are the combination of fluid behavior and magnetic properties [12,13]. The stability of ferrofluids can be achieved through a number of different interactions (e.g. Vander Waals forces, magnetostatic interactions or dipolar attractive interactions) [9,12]. Because of their unusual magnetic, optical and electronic properties, the ferrofluid has been applied in nanotechnology and biomedicine [14,15].

Moreover, the magnetic nanoparticles for the ferrofluid should have a narrowly distributed particle size and nearly spherical shape [16]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as the new class of carbon nanomaterials, are receiving increasing attention because of their high water solubility, small sizes, neglectable toxicity, environmental friendliness, favorable compatibility, high photostability and excellent photocatalytic activities [17–19]. And the tunable surface functionalization of CQDs was widely used in photocatalysis, optoelectronics, chemical and electronic sensor [20–23]. In addition, the presence of many polar moieties (e.g. COOH and OH) is also a hot research point, which can be used to improve the dispersion and hydrophilicity of materials, and meant to broaden their applications [24]. The possibility of carry-over occurred in the process of extraction can be also eliminated under the presence of CQDs. Hence, CQDs have been regarded as promising new materials in separation science, and have been widely applied in different materials and techniques [25–27].

In this study, a novel Fe3O4-OA/CQDs composite was synthesized through a simple one-pot method. The ferrofluid prepared by Fe3O4-OA/CQDs composite was then applied for the determination of phenolic compounds in water and pretreated milk samples. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report on using magnetically CQDs for the preparation of ferrofluids. In addition, the synthesized Fe3O4-OA/CQDs composites were characterized, and various factors affecting performance of the ferrofluid were also investigated in details. The feasibility of the ferrofluid for determination of phenolic compounds in real samples was further studied.

All chemicals were analytical regent-grade and used as received. Bisphenol-A (BPA), bisphenol-AF (BPAF), tetrabromobisphenol-A (TBBPA) and 4-tert-octylphenol (4-TOP), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) and ammonium iron (II) sulfate hexahydrate ((NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O) were provided by Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). 1-Butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate ([C4MIm]PF6), 1-pentyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate ([C5MIm]PF6), 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate ([C6MIm]PF6), 1-heptyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate ([C7MIm]PF6), 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate ([C8MIm]PF6) and 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate ([C10MIm]PF6) were all purchased from Center for Green Chemistry and Catalysis, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Lanzhou, China). The ILs were diluted twice with methanol before use. Ammonia solution (NH3·H2O) (25% w/w), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium acetate (NaAc), citric acid, urea, magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), acetonitrile (ACN), methanol (MeOH), ethanol and oleic acid (OA) were obtained from Tianjin chemical reagent Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Fig. S1 shows the structures of OA, 4-TOP, BPs and ILs. HPLC-grade acetonitrile and methanol were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Standards stock solutions (200μgmL−1) of BPs and 4-TOP were prepared in methanol and stored in the refrigerator at 4–8°C. More diluted working solutions were prepared daily by appropriate dilution of the stock solutions with methanol. Ultrapure water used in the experiment was prepared by using Milli-Q Water Purification System (Millipore Corporation, Bedford, MA, U.S.A.).

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of prepared materials (4000–400cm−1) were recorded on the infrared spectrometer Tensor 27 (Germany, Bruker). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the sorbents were obtained with a Tecnai G2F20 (FEI) electron microscope (200kV, point-to point resolution 0.24nm) (FEI, Holland). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis were obtained using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., U.S.A.) X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data were recorded on D/Max 2200 with Cu Kα radiation (γ=1.5405A) ranging from 2θ=5° to 90° (Rigaku, America). All pH values were detected by a pH-meter Sartorious PB20 (Göttingen, Germany). A vortex agitator (Jintan Guo Wang experimental instrument factory, Jingsu, China), ultrasonic cleaner (KMH1-120W, Shenzhen, China) and a vacuum drying oven (BPZ-5300, Shanghai, China) were used in the experiment.

The concentrations of BPs and 4-TOP were determined with an Agilent 1260 series HPLC system equipped with a UV–vis diode array detector (Agilent Technologies, America). The HPLC separation was performed on a reversed-phase system (Agilent TC-C18 column, 150mm×4.6mm i.d., 5μm) at column temperature of 25°C. The mobile phase was performed as follows: 42% acetonitrile (0–8.0min), 85% acetonitrile (8.0–11.0min). The flow rate was set at 1mLmin−1, and the detector was set to a wavelength of 280nm.

CQDs prepared through hydrothermal method were carried out as followings: 3.0g citric acid and 1.0g urea were dissolved into 20mL water. Then the clear solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (50mL) and maintained at 200°C for 6h. After it had cooled down, the solution was centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 15min to remove the insoluble substances. Finally, the yellow solution was filtered with a 0.22μm membrane and dried overnight at 65°C to obtain CQDs power.

A one-pot synthesis method was employed for the preparation of Fe3O4-OA/CQDs. In brief, CQDs (0.15g) (the optimum amount of CQDs was obtained experimentally) and FeCl3·6H2O (4.10g) were dissolved in 80mL of deionized water under N2 atmosphere with vigorous stirring. Then, the mixture was heated to 80°C, and (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O (2.82g) was added. After stirred for 5min, NH3·H2O (10mL) and OA (2mL) were added drop-wise into the mixture containing Fe3+/CQDs. The reaction was stirred at 80°C for an additional time of 2h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained Fe3O4-OA/CQDs were magnetically separated and washed repeatedly with ethanol for three times to remove the excess OA and CQDs, and vacuum-dried at 60°C for 8h. Finally, the Fe3O4-OA/CQDs were redispersed in deionized water via sonication and then used in the following experiments. The concentration of the prepared Fe3O4-OA/CQDs suspension was set to 24.5mgmL−1.

A range of ILs (C4-C10) (100μL) and Fe3O4-OA/CQDs NPs (300μL) were mixed in a screw cap polyethylene centrifuge tube by sonication for 3min. After Fe3O4-OA/CQDs NPs were dispersed in IL, the obtained ferrofluid was magnetically collected and used for the following extraction process.

The extraction of BPs and 4-TOP by using prepared ferrofluid allowed rapid separation through an external supermagnet. First, a 20mL aliquot of aqueous samples was placed in a 50mL screw cap polyethylene centrifuge tube containing the ferrofluid. Then, the mixture was vortex-shaken immediately for 2min. The solution pH was adjusted to 6.0 with 0.1molL−1 HCl and NaOH. After 5min, the ferrofluid were magnetically separated from the solution with an external magnet. Finally, 1mL of MeOH containing 1% NaOH was used to elute the enriched BPs and 4-TOP from the isolated ferrofluid and 20μL of the extract was injected into HPLC system for analysis.

Milk samples were purchased from the local supermarket (Kunming, China). Lake water samples were collected from the LaoYu River (Kunming, China). Waste water samples were collected from the Chenggong wastewater treatment plant (Kunming, China). Water samples were filtered through a nylon membrane (0.45μm) to remove insoluble substances and acidified to 6 with 0.1molL−1 HCl and NaOH. The water and milk samples were stored at 4°C before analysis and analyzed within 3days.

Milk samples were pretreated to induce protein precipitation before the analysis of the milk samples. For this purpose, NaAc (0.2g), MgSO4 (0.8g) and acetonitrile (4mL) were added in to 10mL of samples and then vortex-shaken for 1min. After that, the mixture was centrifuged at 4000rpm for about 15min to remove the protein and other macromolecules. Then, the supernatant was collected and the procedure was repeated once more to ensure a good protein removal effect. Finally, 10mL of the supernatant were two-fold diluted with deionized water and stored at 4°C and used in the following experiments.

FTIR analysis was applied to investigate the surface functional groups of prepared composites. Fig. 1A shows the FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs, Fe3O4-OA, Fe3O4-OA/CQDs and Fe3O4-OA/CQDs-[C8MIm]PF6 mixtures. In CQDs, the peaks at 1170, 1401, 1552, 1655cm−1 are attributed to the CN, OH stretching vibration, NH and CO stretching, respectively; the broad peak between 3000 and 3500cm−1 indicates the existence of OH and NH [28–31]. In Fe3O4-OA/CQDs, the characteristic bonds of CQDs were also observed. The strong absorption peaks at around 580cm−1 is assigned to FeO stretching vibrations of Fe3O4 NPs, and the absorption peaks at around 2919 and 2850cm−1 were attributed to the CH stretching vibrations. The existence of NH and CN in Fe3O4-OA/CQDs led us to believe that the CQDs were successfully introduced on the surface of Fe3O4-OA NPs. In addition, the new absorption peak at around 841cm−1 in Fe3O4-OA/CQDs-[C8MIm]PF6 mixtures was attributed to PF stretching vibrations [32], which indicated that the [C8MIm]PF6 should be successfully adsorbed on the surface of Fe3O4-OA/CQDs through hydrophobic interactions.

The XPS spectrum was also performed to further investigate the elemental contents and surface groups of Fe3O4-OA/CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1B, the full scan XPS spectrum revealed the predominant presence of carbon (C1s, 284.0eV), nitrogen (N1s, 399.5eV), oxygen (O1s, 531.4eV) and iron (Fe2p, 710.0eV). This indicated that the Fe3O4@OA NPs were successfully functionalized with CQDs. In addition, C1s (Fig. 1B, inset) was split into four peaks at 283.4, 284.7, 286.5, and 287.4eV, which could be attributed to CC/CC, CN, CO and CO, respectively. Therefore, these observations revealed that the hydrophilic groups (COOH, NH2, and OH) should exist on the surface of Fe3O4-OA/CQDs, which concurred with the FTIR results.

Fig. 2 shows the TEM and size distribution of CQDs, Fe3O4-OA and Fe3O4-OA/CQDs. The TEM image in Fig. 2A (inset) revealed that the diameter of CQDs are in the range of 3–5nm. As can been seen from Fig. 2B, the prepared Fe3O4-OA/CQDs NPs possessed a uniform size with a particle size of about 5–10nm. Compared with the Fe3O4-OA NPs, the particles size and morphology of Fe3O4-OA/CQDs composites were well changed, which are favorable for the dispersion and stability of the ferrofluid. Fig. S2 shows the XRD patterns of CQDs and Fe3O4-OA/CQDs. The peaks at 2θ=30.2, 35.5, 43.3, 49.6, 50.3, and 57.2° are consistent with the pattern of the Fe3O4 NPs according to the JCPDS card. The XRD pattern of CQDs (Fig. S2, inset) displayed a peak at 2θ=23.5°, which is in agreement with the graphitic structure [33].

In general, the character of the coatings and the size of the MNPs were the main parameters for the preparation of ferrofluids. Firstly, for the synthesis of MNPs, it is necessary to cover the Fe3O4 NPs with an appropriate layer of the surfactant to ensure the stability of ferrofluids. Secondly, the size of MNPs used in ferrofluids is between 5 and 10nm and the MNPs should have a spherical shape. Moreover, since the used [C8MIm]PF6 have poor hydrophilicity and dispersibility, some techniques should be applied to increase the dispersion. CQDs, as one of the carbon nanomaterials, show small particles (about 5nm) and excellent dispersing capability in aqueous solutions. The hydrophilic groups on the surface of the CQDs caused it to be good candidate for new nanomaterials with enhanced hydrophilicity. Therefore, OA (as a surfactant) and CQDs were selected for the synthesis of magnetic NPs to prepare and stabilize the ferrofluid in the present study.

In order to clarify the function of CQDs on extraction efficiencies, Fe3O4-OA (used to prepare ferrofluid I) and Fe3O4-OA/CQDs (used to prepare ferrofluid II) were combined with [C8MIm]PF6 for the extraction of BPs and 4-TOP, respectively. The results are shown in Fig. 3A, which represent superiority of Fe3O4-OA/CQDs than Fe3O4-OA. Moreover, the precision and stability of the ferrofluid I and ferrofluid II were evaluated by analyzing the spiked samples under the same conditions. As can been seen, the relative standard deviations (RSDs) (n =8) of ferrofluids (inset of Fig. 3A) were clearly reduced from 8.3–5.5% to 1.1–2.1%, which indicated that the CQDs increased the reproducibility and precision of methods. A t-test also revealed that the extraction efficiency of these two ferrofluids is significantly different (p <0.01). The introduction of CQDs increased the hydrophilicity of the Fe3O4-OA/CQDs. Therefore, the ferrofluid could be better dispersed in aqueous solution. Ultimately, the extraction efficiency and reproducibility of the method were also improved.

In addition, the results (Fig. 3A) showed that the extraction efficiencies of BPs and 4-TOP using Fe3O4-OA/CQDs NPs alone were 36.7%, 52.24%, 25.30% and 39.53%, respectively. And, it was not difficult to find that the ILs had a significant influence on the recovery of BPs and 4-TOP. In literatures, hydrogen bonds are known to form between fluorine of ILs and hydroxyl of phenolic compounds or glucocorticoids [34,35]. This could be due to the fact that H-bonding interaction was formed between the hydroxyl of phenolic compounds and the fluorine of [C8MIm]PF6.

In the present study, several parameters including the type of ILs, the amount of Fe3O4-OA/CQDs NPs, the pH, the extraction time, and the type of elution were examined to obtain the optimum experimental conditions. Experiments were carried out with water samples spiked with 100μgL−1 of BPs and 4-TOP.

In order to find the type of ILs, extraction procedures were performed using Fe3O4-OA/CQDs NPs combined with six different kinds of hydrophobic ILs (including [C4MIm]PF6 (C4), [C5MIm]PF6 (C5), [C6MIm]PF6 (C6), [C7MIm]PF6 (C7), [C8MIm]PF6 (C8), [C10MIm]PF6 (C10)). Fig. 3B shows the influence of the carbon chain length of ILs on the extraction efficiency. The extraction efficiencies for BPs and 4-TOP obviously increased as the chain length increased from 4 to 8, and the extraction efficiencies reached a plateau with alkyl chains of >8. The results revealed that a proper carbon chain length might provide enough hydrophobic interactions between ILs and MNPs, so, the magnetic particles were stably suspended in carrier liquid and the ferrofluid maintained its integrity during the extraction process. Therefore, [C8MIm]PF6 was the most suitable carrier liquid and selected as a carrier to prepare ferrofluid for the extraction of BPs and 4-TOP from water and milk samples.

The amount of Fe3O4-OA/CQDs is an important factor that directly affects the volume of confined [C8MIm]PF6. In the study, different volumes of Fe3O4-OA/CQDs suspension (20–500μL) were added to a 50mL screw cap polyethylene centrifuge tube containing 100μL of [C8MIm]PF6 to prepare ferrofluids. As shown in Fig. S3-a, the extraction efficiencies of BPs and 4-TOP enhanced with the dosage of Fe3O4-OA/CQDs NPs increased, but above 300μL, the extraction efficiencies remained a constant level, therefore, 7.35mg (300μL) was selected in the following studies.

Generally, the pH of the solution is one of the prime factors which determined the surface binding sites of the sorbent and the present state of analytes. The influence of pH on the extraction of BPs and 4-TOP from aqueous samples were investigated within the pH range of 2.0–12.0 using 0.1molL−1 HCl and NaOH. The results (Fig. S3-b) showed that with changing pH in the range of 2.0–6.0, the extraction efficiency of BPs and 4-TOP increased by about 10% to 20%. Then, in pH range 8.0–12.0, the extraction efficiency of BPs and 4-TOP decreased significantly. Generally, Fe3O4 NPs would dissolve in the acidic solution. However, a slightly influence on the extraction was observed in the pH range 2.0–6.0. It might be due to the protection of oleic acid covered on the surface of Fe3O4 NPs. The acid dissociation constant pKa of BPA and 4-TOP are 9.73 and 9.5, respectively [36,37]. And pKa of BPAF and TBBPA are around 7.4–8.5 [38]. At high pH level (pH>pKa), the phenolic compounds remains in dissociated state and electrostatic repulsion interaction between dissociated analytes molecules and negatively charged oleic acid molecules cause the decrease of the extraction efficiency. Based on this fact, pH6.0 was selected in the following studies.

Fig. S3-c shows the influence of extraction time on the extraction of BPs and 4-TOP. The influence of extraction time was studied in the range of 1–30min. The result indicated that the maximum value of extraction efficiency was obtained at 5min. The further increase in extraction time gave no additional increase in extraction efficiencies. Therefore, the extraction time for the extraction of BPs and 4-TOP was set at 5min.

In general, due to salting-out effect, addition of salt reduces the solubility of the analyte in the aqueous phase and improves the extraction efficiency. The addition of salt can also affect the ionic strength, and the effect of ionic strength was evaluated by adding the different concentration of sodium chloride (0–1molL−1). But, the result (data not given) showed that the addition of salt had no significant influence on the extraction. Therefore, there was no need to adjust the ionic strength of the samples.

It is very important to obtain a suitable eluent to improve the extraction efficiency of the method. The ILs used in this method can be dissolved in MeOH and ACN. And the analysis of the effect of pH value exhibited that the extraction efficiency would decrease in alkaline conditions. Thus, four kinds of desorption solvents, including MeOH, MeOH containing 1% NaOH, ACN and ACN containing 1% NaOH, were used for the elution of BPs and 4-TOP. As shown in Fig. S3-d, the results indicated that the maximum extraction efficiencies were obtained when MeOH containing 1% NaOH was used. Therefore, MeOH containing 1% NaOH was selected as the optimum desorption solvent. In addition, the volume of elution from 0.5 to 3mL were also investigated with the constant experimental conditions. The experimental results showed that 1mL of MeOH containing 1% NaOH was enough to elute the extracted BPs and 4-TOP.

Under the optimized conditions, the analytical characteristics for BPs and 4-TOP using the proposed method were evaluated and summarized in Table S1. All the experiments were carried out in triplicate. A good linearity was obtained in the range of 2–1000μgL−1 with the determination coefficients (R 2) of 0.9984–0.9996. The limit of detections (LODs), based on S/N=3, ranged from 0.09 to 0.17μgL−1. The intra-day precision was determined over five replicates in one day, spiked water samples at two concentration levels (20 and 100μgL−1). The inter-day precision was determined by analyzing the water samples, spiked at the same level per work day, over a period of three days. The intra-day and inter-day precision (RSD%), as presented in Table S1, were in the range 1.5–2.6% and 1.6–2.8%, respectively. The result indicated the current method had an acceptable precision for the determination of BPs and 4-TOP from aqueous samples.

In literatures, different extractants and adsorbents could be used for the extraction of phenolic compounds [35,39–42]. A comparison of analytical characteristics of the present method and other methods is shown in Table 1 . For water samples, the recoveries for the phenolic compounds obtained by ferrofluid, octanol and HPDMIP were all above 90% except for fiber-coating materials. It was not difficult to find that the LODs of the proposed method were the lowest, and the extraction time is further decreased by using the ferrofluid. Compared to the adsorbents used in the process of SPE, DMIP-SPE and SPME, the material used in the proposed method is easier and simpler to synthesize. Although the preparation of the ferrofluid takes about 3min, it is worth noting that the proposed method is simpler, faster and easier to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the method in water and milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the proposed method was studied by extracting BPs and 4-TOP from waste water, lake water and milk samples. The samples were spiked at different concentrations of the BPs and 4-TOP (20, and 300μgL−1). The detail data are listed in Table 2 . The results showed that none of the BPs and 4-TOP were detected in lake water and milk samples. Additionally, the spiking recovery of BPA, BPAF, TBBPA and 4-TOP from water and milk samples were 95.5–98.8%, 94.5–97.9%, 94.5–101.3%, and 94.6–102.1%, respectively, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were in range of 1.7 to 4.1%. The typical HPLC chromatograms of a blank and a spiked milk samples are shown in Fig. 4 .

In this study, the novel, green and facile Fe3O4-OA/CQDs composites were synthesized via a one-step method and used to prepare the ferrofluid for the extraction of BPs and 4-TOP from water and milk samples. The ferrofluid was composed of Fe3O4-OA/CQDs and [C8MIm]PF6. Following the introduction of CQDs, the ferrofluid exhibited good dispersion performance, good extraction efficiency and low RSDs, which indicated that the CQDs could be used to enhance the dispersibility of materials and improve the reproducibility and precision of method. The high viscosity of the [C8MIm]PF6 could be reduced, and the centrifugation step for phase separation could be also omitted. Additionally, the study showed that the chain length of ILs had a significant influence on the extraction, and a proper chain length might provide enough Van der Waals interactions between Fe3O4-OA/CQDs and ILs. This work revealed a new way to enhance the extraction efficiency and provided a promising method for the extraction of BPs and 4-TOP.
This work was greatly supported by Kunming University of Science and Technology.
The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest. This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2018.04.036.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Concentrations and profiles of bisphenol A and other bisphenol analogues in foodstuffs from the United States and their implications for human exposure
Trends in analytical methodologies for the determination of alkylphenols and bisphenol A in water samples
Impact of endocrine-disrupting compounds (EDCs) on female reproductive health
Combination of supercritical fluid extraction with dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction for extraction of organophosphorus pesticides from soil and marine sediment samples
Ionic liquid-based solvent bar microextraction for determination of organophosphorus pesticides in water samples
Supramolecular solvents combined with layered double hydroxide-coated magnetic nanoparticles for extraction of bisphenols and 4-tert-octylphenol from fruit juices
Analytical applications of room-temperature ionic liquids: a review of recent efforts
